questions.question: What is a tracheostomy?
options: A type of hearing aid worn around the neck
A plaster placed over the mouth to help someone breathe
A machine that breathes on the person's behalf at all times
A surgically created opening in the front of the neck that goes directly into the windpipe, below the voice box

questions.question: What is the opening of a tracheostomy in the neck called?
options: A valve
A cuff
A stoma
A cannula

questions.question: What is the main purpose of this Tracheostomy Awareness course?
options: To qualify you to suction a tracheostomy
To train you to perform tracheostomy surgery
To qualify you to change tracheostomy tubes
To raise your awareness so you can support safely and recognise an emergency

questions.question: In normal breathing, what does the nose and upper airway do to the air we breathe in?
options: Adds secretions to it
Cools, dries and colours it
Removes all the oxygen from it
Warms, moistens and filters it

questions.question: Why might a person with a tracheostomy be unable to speak normally?
options: The person simply chooses not to talk to carers
A tracheostomy damages the person's ability to think
Air enters below the voice box, so less air passes across it to make sound
The tube makes the mouth too dry to form words

questions.question: What is a cuffed tracheostomy tube?
options: A tube that is only ever used for children
A tube with no balloon that lets air pass around it
A tube with an inflatable balloon around its lower end that can help seal the airway
A tube made entirely of soft cloth

questions.question: What is an uncuffed tracheostomy tube?
options: A tube that cannot be removed once fitted
A tube with no balloon, allowing air to pass around it
A tube with a large balloon that always seals the airway
A tube that automatically suctions the airway

questions.question: Who decides which type of tracheostomy tube a person has?
options: Any care worker on shift
Trained clinical staff, chosen to suit the person's needs
The person themselves without any advice
The person's family members

questions.question: What is a removable inner tube (inner cannula)?
options: A part that sits inside the outer tube and can be taken out for cleaning while the outer tube keeps the airway open
The balloon at the end of a cuffed tube
A spare tube kept in the office cupboard
The tie that holds the tube around the neck

questions.question: Why is the removable inner tube described as a safety feature?
options: It makes the tube completely waterproof
It allows the person to speak completely normally
It can be removed quickly to help clear the airway if secretions start to block it
It means the person never needs humidification

questions.question: At an awareness level, how does a laryngectomy differ from a tracheostomy?
options: After a laryngectomy the neck opening is the only airway, with no connection through the nose or mouth to the lungs
There is no difference; the words mean the same thing
A laryngectomy is always a temporary tube removed within a week
A person with a laryngectomy can always breathe normally through their nose

questions.question: With a tracheostomy (as opposed to a laryngectomy), what is still true of the airway?
options: The person can never breathe through the tube
The nose and mouth are permanently sealed off
The voice box has been surgically removed
The voice box is still in place and there may still be a connection between mouth, nose and windpipe

questions.question: Why does the difference between tracheostomy and laryngectomy matter to a responder?
options: It changes the person's mealtimes
It changes the colour of the person's clothing
It has no effect on how an emergency is handled
It changes how oxygen and help can reach the lungs

questions.question: Why does the airway of a person with a tracheostomy tend to dry out?
options: Because the room is always too humid
Because the person drinks too much fluid
Because the tube is made of metal
Because air enters below the nose and is not warmed and moistened before reaching the windpipe

questions.question: What is humidification, in the context of tracheostomy care?
options: Added moisture to replace what the nose would normally provide to the air
A way of making the room smell pleasant for visitors
A type of emergency phone call
A method of drying out the person's secretions

questions.question: What can happen to secretions if the airway is left cold and dry without humidification?
options: They make the person speak more clearly
They turn into clean water
They become thick, sticky and harder to clear, and can block the tube
They disappear completely

questions.question: How should the skin around the stoma be kept?
options: Wet and covered at all times
Coated in talcum powder
Clean and dry
Left completely uncovered and unwatched

questions.question: A common guide for how tight tracheostomy ties should be is that they are:
options: So tight that no finger can fit underneath
So loose the tube can easily fall out
Removed completely during the day
Snug, but you can still slide a finger comfortably underneath

questions.question: Which of these could obstruct a tracheostomy tube opening and should be kept clear of it?
options: A call bell placed within reach
A pen and paper for communication
Bedding, clothing, a scarf or the person's own chin when they slump forward
The person's prescribed humidification

questions.question: Why must water be kept out of a tracheostomy tube during washing?
options: Because water makes the ties change colour
Because water improves the person's speech
Because water is needed to inflate the cuff
Because the tube leads straight into the windpipe, so anything entering it goes towards the lungs

questions.question: Which of these should be kept away from the tracheostomy opening?
options: The person's call bell
The heat and moisture exchanger prescribed in the care plan
The person's picture or alphabet board
Aerosols, talcum powder, and fluff or fibres from tissues and blankets

questions.question: Because speech is affected, which communication approach is recommended?
options: Only speak to the person's family and ignore the person
Agree simple signals and keep a pen and paper, picture or alphabet board, or a tablet within reach
Refuse to communicate until speech returns
Rush the person and pretend to understand when you do not

questions.question: What is suctioning of a tracheostomy?
options: Blowing air into the tube to inflate the lungs
Giving the person a drink of water
Wiping the outside of the neck with a dry cloth
Using a thin catheter and a machine to gently draw secretions out of the tube and airway to keep it clear

questions.question: Why is suctioning only carried out by specifically trained staff?
options: Because it can be uncomfortable and, done wrongly, can harm the airway, cause bleeding or infection, or drop the person's oxygen
Because it is against the person's religion
Because it is boring and time-consuming
Because the equipment is very expensive to buy

questions.question: You are supporting someone with a tracheostomy who you think may need suctioning. What should you do?
options: Wait several hours to see whether it clears on its own
Recognise they may need their airway cleared and get a trained colleague to help without delay; do not suction yourself
Tell the person to hold their breath until a nurse arrives
Carry out the suctioning yourself as quickly as you can

questions.question: Which of these is a sign that a person's airway may be clogging up with secretions?
options: Quiet, easy, comfortable breathing
The person singing loudly
The person's ties being correctly secured
Noisy, rattly or bubbly breathing, or breathing that becomes harder work

questions.question: A tracheostomy emergency is usually about the airway being:
options: Blocked, or the tube being displaced (out of position or fallen out)
Cleaned and freshly dressed
Perfectly clear and comfortable
Slightly dry but working normally

questions.question: A person with a tracheostomy suddenly has lips, face or skin turning pale, grey or blue while struggling to breathe. What does this colour change mean?
options: The humidification is working well
The ties are slightly too loose
The person is not getting enough oxygen, and it is an emergency
The person is feeling too warm

questions.question: What is your single most important action if a person with a tracheostomy cannot breathe?
options: Wait to see whether their breathing improves before doing anything
Try to change the tracheostomy tube yourself before help arrives
Leave the person to look for the care plan in the office
Get urgent help immediately - call 999 (or have someone call) and tell them the person has a tracheostomy

questions.question: On the bedhead sign, which colour is used for a tracheostomy and which for a laryngectomy?
options: Blue for tracheostomy, yellow for laryngectomy
Black for tracheostomy, white for laryngectomy
Green for tracheostomy, red for laryngectomy
Red for tracheostomy, green for laryngectomy

questions.question: What does the bedhead sign show a responder at a glance?
options: The price of the tracheostomy equipment
What kind of airway the person has and key details a responder needs
The person's favourite meals
The visiting hours for the family

questions.question: When you call 999 for a tracheostomy emergency, what vital information should you give the call handler?
options: Nothing; you should just hang up and wait
Only your own name and job title
The person's favourite television programme
That the person has a tracheostomy, or a laryngectomy, so the right response comes

questions.question: While waiting for trained help for someone whose airway may need clearing, what should you do?
options: Stay with the person, keep them calm, help them into a good breathing position, and ensure nothing covers the tube
Leave the person alone and go for a break
Cover the tube with a scarf to keep them warm
Attempt to suction the airway yourself

questions.question: If you are ever unsure whether a task is within your role, what is the safe answer?
options: Stop and ask a nurse or senior colleague rather than guess
Ask the person to decide for you
Do the task quickly before anyone notices
Guess and hope it turns out fine

questions.question: This Tracheostomy Awareness course authorises you to suction the airway and change tracheostomy tubes.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care worker must never suction, change or reinsert a tracheostomy tube, or change an inner tube, unless specifically trained, assessed as competent, and delegated the task in the care plan.
options: True
False

questions.question: Tracheostomy ties should be so tight that no finger can fit underneath them.
options: True
False

questions.question: Noisy, rattly breathing or visible secretions at the tube can be a sign that a person's airway needs clearing and should be reported.
options: True
False

questions.question: Adding humidification means the person no longer needs to drink any fluids.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should never leave a person who is struggling to breathe alone.
options: True
False

questions.question: After a laryngectomy the person can still breathe normally through their nose and mouth.
options: True
False

questions.question: In a tracheostomy emergency you do not need to work out the exact cause before getting urgent help.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are effects of air bypassing the nose and mouth in a person with a tracheostomy? (Select all that apply)
options: Secretions can become thick and harder to clear
The person's sense of smell is permanently improved
The person's thinking and memory are damaged
The air is no longer warmed, moistened and filtered, so the airway can become dry
Less air passes across the voice box, so the person may not speak normally

questions.question: Which of these should be kept away from a tracheostomy opening? (Select all that apply)
options: Talcum powder and aerosols
A call bell within reach
Water and sprays
Fluff and fibres from tissues, blankets or fluffy clothing
The person's prescribed heat and moisture exchanger

questions.question: Which of the following are warning signs of a tracheostomy emergency? (Select all that apply)
options: The person becoming drowsy, confused or unresponsive
Obvious difficulty breathing that is fast, laboured or noisy
The lips, face or skin turning pale, grey or blue
The person chatting comfortably and breathing quietly
The person clutching at their neck or unable to make a sound

questions.question: Which tasks must a care worker NOT carry out unless specifically trained, assessed competent and delegated? (Select all that apply)
options: Making sure nothing is covering the tube opening
Changing or reinserting the tracheostomy tube
Removing, cleaning or replacing an inner tube
Suctioning the airway
Keeping the person calm and reassured

questions.question: What can a care worker do to support someone with a tracheostomy within their role? (Select all that apply)
options: Providing comfort, dignity and good communication
Deciding which type of tube the person should have
Changing the tracheostomy tube whenever they judge it necessary
Attentive observation and reporting concerns early
Keeping the airway clear and following the care plan

questions.question: Which signs might tell you a person's airway is clogging with secretions and needs clearing? (Select all that apply)
options: The person becoming restless or anxious
Quiet, effortless, comfortable breathing
Bubbly, rattly or noisy breathing
Coughing repeatedly or visible secretions at the tube
The person signalling that they cannot clear it

questions.question: In a tracheostomy emergency, which actions should you take? (Select all that apply)
options: Stay with the person and follow the emergency plan and call handler's instructions
Attempt suctioning you are not trained for
Tell the call handler the person has a tracheostomy or laryngectomy
Call 999 without delay, or have someone else dial while you stay with the person
Spend time trying to fix the tube yourself when someone cannot breathe

questions.question: A tracheostomy dries out the airway, so ___ is needed to keep it moist.
The opening of a tracheostomy in the front of the neck is called a ___.
In a tracheostomy emergency where someone cannot breathe, your single most important action is to call ___ for urgent help.
On a bedhead sign, a ___ sign is used for a tracheostomy and a red sign for a laryngectomy.
Because anything entering the tube travels towards the ___, water and sprays must be kept away from the opening.
